request.question: Who was the singer of She's Not Just a Pretty Face and Forever and for Always?

title: Impatient (Anna Abreu song)
Dolly Sinatra
She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Just a Pretty Face?
Forever and for Always
Face Facts
On to Victory (album)
Never Trust a Pretty Face
The Pretty Things Are Going to Hell
Pretty Face
sentences: "Impatient" is a song by Finnish singer Anna Abreu from her third studio album, "Just a Pretty Face?
Abreu co-wrote the song with longtime collaborator Rauli Eskolin (known professionally as Rake), and Patric Sarin.
Eskolin also produced the song, while Sarin provided additional backing vocals.
"Impatient" is a Pop ballad.
The song was released on 9 November 2009 in Finland, as the album's second single.
Dolly Sinatra (born Natalina Maria Vittoria Garaventa; December 26, 1896 – January 6, 1977) was the mother of American singer Frank Sinatra.
She was born in Lumarzo (Province of Genoa), in northern Italy; she immigrated to the United States when she was two months old.
When she was a child, her pretty face earned her the nickname "Dolly".
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.
It was the fourth country single from her album "Up!
The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.
Twain had announced that "Nah!"
would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.
It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.
The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.
It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.
Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.
The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.
The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.
Just a Pretty Face?
is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.
The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
"Forever and for Always" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain and it was the third country single from her fourth studio album "Up!
The song was written by Mutt Lange and Twain.
The single was released in the United States radio on April 7, 2003.
The video debuted on Country Music Television on April 26, 2003.
The song was also used for Febreze: Scentstories promotion, in which Twain took part.
In 2006, "Forever and for Always" was certified gold for 500,000 digital downloads in the U.S. by the RIAA.
Face Facts is a 1980 album from glam rock band T. Roth and Another Pretty Face.
Bon Jovi drummer Tico Torres plays on the album.

On to Victory is the ninth studio album recorded by the English rock band Humble Pie and the first with a new lineup including vocalist and guitarist Steve Marriott, Drummer Jerry Shirley, vocalist and guitarist Bobby Tench from The Jeff Beck Group, and American bassist Anthony "Sooty" Jones.
They recorded "Fool for a Pretty Face", which Marriott had written earlier and the song proved good enough for them to secure a recording contract with Atco in 1980.
In UK their material was released by Jet Records, owned by the former Small Faces manager Don Arden.

The album peaked #60 on the "Billboard" 200 album chart "Fool for a Pretty Face" was released as a single and reached #58 on the US singles charts and a promotional tour followed as part of "The Rock'n Roll Marathon", supporting Ted Nugent and Aerosmith
Never Trust a Pretty Face is the third studio album by French singer Amanda Lear, released by the West German label Ariola Records in 1979.
The album included notable hit singles "The Sphinx" and "Fashion Pack (Studio 54)", and turned out a commercial and critical success.
"The Pretty Things Are Going to Hell" is a song written by David Bowie and Reeves Gabrels for the album "Hours" in 1999.
The first single release from the album in Australia and Japan, while the rest of the world got "Thursday's Child" as their first single.
The first appearance of the song was on the soundtrack of the film "Stigmata" in 1999.
It charted and peaked at No. 30 in Japan.
The song's title takes influence from the song "Your Pretty Face Is Going to Hell" by The Stooges from their album "Raw Power" produced by Bowie himself.
Pretty Face (プリティフェイス , Puriti Feisu ) is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Japanese author Yasuhiro Kano.

The story revolves around a high school boy who suffers an accident and having his face reconstructed in the form of his crush, attempts to impersonate a girl whom his crush mistakes for her twin sister.
A common theme in the series is the loving relationships the characters share for each other, whether they be between siblings, friends, or lovers as well as Karma.
It was first serialized in Jump Comic's "Weekly Shōnen Jump" by Shueisha between May 2002 and June 2003, finally coming to an end at chapter fifty-two.
For North America Viz Media acquired the rights to release an English translation of the series.

title: She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Forever and for Always

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

It was the fourth country single from her album "Up! ".  The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.  Twain had announced that "Nah!"  would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.  It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.  The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.  It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.  "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.  Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.  The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.  The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.

Just a Pretty Face? | Just a Pretty Face?  is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.  The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
Not Just a Pretty Face (Rowan Atkinson album) | Not Just a Pretty Face is a comedy album by British comedian Rowan Atkinson.

Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces | Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces is the seventh studio album by British jazz-rock band If, released in 1974.  The band was now back on Capitol Records for U.S. distribution.
Always is Forever Tour | Always is Forever Tour was a concerts tour by American singer Diana Ross in 1999.

Always & Forever (Randy Travis album) | Always & Forever is the second studio album by country music singer Randy Travis.  It was released on April 4, 1987 by Warner Bros.  Records.  Released from this album were the singles "Too Gone Too Long", "I Won't Need You Anymore (Always and Forever)", "Forever and Ever, Amen" and "I Told You So", all of which reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs charts.

Always and Forever (Kostas Martakis song) | "Always and Forever" is a song and CD single by Greek singer Kostas Martakis.  It was Martakis's entry in a race to be the Greek representative for the Eurovision Song Contest 2008.

response.summary_1:
- [1] "co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain." — establishes singer of "She's Not Just a Pretty Face." (quoted)
- [1] passage refers to "'Forever and for Always'" as a prior single by Twain — establishes that "Forever and for Always" is also by Shania Twain. (paraphrase)
- Bridge entity: Shania Twain — unambiguous (present in [1]); no missing facts.
- Final answer (supported by passages): Both songs were sung by Shania Twain.
